expectations.text: Recommends HSL format with explanation of why
expectations.evidence: HSL appears only under 'Method B: Manual HSL Approach' as a secondary option after hex-based tool recommendations. No explanation of why HSL is superior — just a mechanical description of the hue/saturation/lightness axes. Primary recommendation is hex-based tools.

expectations.text: Explains a multi-shade scale (e.g., 50 to 900, or 9 shades) with different use cases for light vs dark shades
expectations.evidence: 'Build a 9–10 Step Scale' with a full table from Blue 50 to Blue 900, each row annotated with a use case (e.g., 'Page backgrounds, tinted sections', 'Primary CTA buttons')

expectations.text: Mentions saturation needs to be adjusted as lightness changes to prevent washed-out shades
expectations.evidence: Mentions hue rotation ('Slightly shift hue warmer at dark end, cooler at light end') but never addresses saturation adjustment. The perceptual lightness issue is not explained.

expectations.text: Mentions the palette needs more than primary blue: including greys and/or semantic colors (danger, success, warning)
expectations.evidence: 'Complementary Colors: Neutrals (gray scale with slight blue tint); Semantic Colors: Success/Green, Warning/Amber, Error/Red'

suggestions.reason: Consider adding an assertion about 'warm greys for warm brands, cool greys for cool brands' — the with_skill response explicitly teaches this rule which is a meaningful discriminator

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions are working well. Assertions 1 and 3 are good discriminators between generic color knowledge and Refactoring UI methodology.
